original_hypothesis.title: Permanent distribution lock if a single recipient/token transfer reverts (blacklist/pause/nonstandard ERC20), blocking all transfers/mints/burns
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: distribute() sets LockedForDistribution=true in _beginDistribution(), then performs external ERC20 `transfer()` calls inside nested loops. If any transfer reverts (e.g., token is paused/blacklists a holder; token is nonstandard and reverts on ABI decoding; insufficient balance due to rebasing), the entire distribute() call reverts and the distribution remains locked (LockedForDistribution stays true because state changes prior to revert are rolled back to pre-call, but the lock can still be re-entered and get stuck across attempts if every attempt fails at the same holder/token pair). Practically, if one holder cannot receive a given distributable token, finishing a distribution may be impossible, freezing all ERC20 transfers due to the lock requirement in _beforeTokenTransfer().
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Real-world stablecoins (USDT/USDC) can blacklist addresses; many tokens can be paused. Any single problematic recipient or misconfigured distributable token can brick distributions and, by design, block all transfers/mints/burns until distribution completes.
deployment_assumptions: At least one distributableERC20 either (a) reverts on transfer for some recipients (blacklist/pause) or (b) is nonstandard (no boolean return) causing revert
At least one such recipient exists in holders[] and is approved at time of transfer attempt
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: Project documentation states distributable tokens include vetted stablecoins (e.g., USDC, DAI, USDT) but no on-chain enforcement of token compliance; distribute() uses raw IERC20.transfer() without SafeERC20.

proof_of_signal.pattern: distribute(): nested loop calls `IERC20(distributableERC20s[j]).transfer(recipient, entitlement)` with no SafeERC20 wrapper; distribution lock blocks all transfers via _beforeTokenTransfer()

preconditions: LockedForDistribution can be entered (MinDistributionPeriod elapsed, supply>0, holders>0)
At least one (recipient, token) pair causes `transfer()` to revert consistently
No admin emergency-unlock function exists (none in code)

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: external token transfers inside distribution loop without per-recipient error handling; hard global lock on token movements while distribution in progress
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: one bad recipient/token can prevent completion forever, freezing ERC20 operations
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No try/catch around transfer, no skip-on-revert strategy, no admin forced unlock

analysis_questions: Is LockedForDistribution ever set true and persisted across calls, or always reverted on failing transfers (confirm state rollback behavior and whether partial progress can be resumed)?
Can distribution be resumed after revert, or does it always fail at the same index because nextDistributionRecipient doesn't advance?
What happens if a token returns false vs reverts vs no return value under Solidity 0.8.12 ABI expectations?
validation_requirements: Simulate a distributable token that reverts for one recipient; ensure distribute() cannot progress past that recipient
Test with a no-return ERC20 mock (USDT-like) to see if it reverts and bricks distributions
Confirm that mint/burn/transfer are blocked while LockedForDistribution is true and that there is no recovery path

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker ensures their address is in holders[] (and allowlisted) then triggers conditions where transfer to them reverts for a chosen distributable token (e.g., by getting blacklisted on that token, or token is paused). Anyone calling distribute() will always revert at the attacker’s index, making it impossible to complete distributions. Because transfers/mints/burns are blocked during distribution, the ERC20 becomes effectively frozen whenever a distribution attempt is made and cannot be completed.
prerequisites: Presence of a restrictive/blacklisting/pausable distributable token OR nonstandard ERC20 added to distributableERC20s

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Protocol-wide freeze of ERC20 transferability and supply operations; distributions cannot be completed so revenue cannot be paid out
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All ERC20 holders and protocol operators
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Operational only (change distributableERC20s, remove holder, or migrate), but may be blocked by lock/logic depending on exact failure mode

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No negative tests for blacklisted recipients, paused tokens, or nonstandard ERC20 return values during distribute().

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Not primary; failure is deterministic revert
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block; does not require transaction ordering
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable repeated reverts at same distribution index; LockedForDistribution toggling behavior to verify

validation_result.mechanism: DoS via Failed External Call in Locked State
validation_result.impact_summary: A reverting ERC20 transfer during distribution permanently locks the contract, freezing all token transfers, mints, and burns.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The `distribute()` function iterates over token holders and pushes rewards using `IERC20.transfer()`. If any transfer reverts (e.g., due to a paused token, a blacklisted recipient, or a non-standard token like USDT that lacks a return value), the entire transaction reverts. 

If this revert occurs during a subsequent batch of a partial distribution (where `LockedForDistribution` was set to `true` in a previous transaction), the state rolls back to the start of the transaction, leaving `LockedForDistribution` permanently `true`. This global lock causes `_beforeTokenTransfer()` to revert, completely freezing all ERC20 transfers, mints, and burns. 

Even if the revert happens on the first batch (leaving the lock `false`), `_isPastMinDistributionPeriod()` remains true, permanently blocking mints and burns. While the admin can manually intervene using `disapproveHolder()` or `setDistributableERC20s()`, this requires manual operational overhead, skips rewards for remaining holders, and can be repeatedly triggered by a malicious user who intentionally gets blacklisted.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code uses a push-payment pattern inside a loop without `try/catch` or `SafeERC20`. The global `LockedForDistribution` state variable creates a hard dependency between the success of external calls and the core functionality of the ERC20 token.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The external `transfer` call is made without `try/catch` or `SafeERC20`. If it reverts, the entire loop and transaction revert, preventing the distribution from advancing.
This hook blocks all token transfers, mints, and burns while `LockedForDistribution` is true. If a distribution is permanently stuck, the token is completely frozen.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path:
1. `distribute()` is called, triggering `_beginDistribution()` which sets `LockedForDistribution = true`.
2. The first batch of distributions succeeds, saving the locked state.
3. `distribute()` is called again for the next batch.
4. An `IERC20.transfer()` call reverts (e.g., recipient is blacklisted).
5. The transaction reverts, rolling back to the locked state.
6. `_beforeTokenTransfer()` now reverts all transfers because `LockedForDistribution` is true.

deployment_assumptions_checked: At least one distributableERC20 either (a) reverts on transfer for some recipients (blacklist/pause) or (b) is nonstandard (no boolean return) causing revert
At least one such recipient exists in holders[] and is approved at time of transfer attempt
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment scripts use mock tokens (`TestERC20A`, `TestERC20B`, `TestERC20C`) whose source code is not provided. It is unverifiable whether these specific mocks revert or lack a return value, though the protocol intends to support standard stablecoins which may have these properties.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: A malicious user acquires tokens and gets their address blacklisted by one of the distributable ERC20s (e.g., USDC). When a distribution period starts, the first batch of distributions is processed, locking the contract. When the distribution reaches the attacker's index, the `transfer()` reverts. The distribution can never complete, and the LiquidInfrastructureERC20 token is permanently frozen.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. The entire ERC20 token becomes frozen (no transfers, mints, or burns). While admin intervention is possible, it requires altering protocol state mid-distribution and causes loss of rewards for users.
recommended_mitigations: Use a pull-payment (withdrawal) pattern for distributions instead of pushing tokens in a loop.
If push-payment is required, wrap the external `transfer` call in a `try/catch` block to gracefully handle reverts and record failed payouts for later manual claiming.
Use OpenZeppelin's `SafeERC20` to handle non-standard tokens like USDT that do not return a boolean value.
validation_result.replication_plan:
1. Deploy `LiquidInfrastructureERC20` with a mock ERC20 that reverts on transfer to a specific address.
2. Add the specific address to the `holders` array.
3. Call `distribute()` in batches such that the first batch succeeds and the second batch includes the reverting address.
4. Observe that the second batch reverts, leaving `LockedForDistribution = true`.
5. Attempt to transfer the LiquidInfrastructureERC20 token and observe it reverts.
